title: Nonlocal Teams and Information Structures
authors: Drishti Baruah, Sachin Teli, Ankur A. Kulkarni
abstract: We look at Bell inequalities from the lens of information structures in stochastic teams. We consider the usual CHSH game and a dynamic variant of the same to study how various classes of strategies, classical, projective and quantum, behave under team theoretic solution concepts. We find that projective strategies (where each player performs projective measurements) enjoy important properties in the usual CHSH game, but they do not carry over to its dynamic version. These results shed light on the delicate interplay of information structure in quantum strategies and the fragility of some well known ideas under changes of information structure.
summary: This theoretical work analyzes Bell inequalities by framing them as team optimization problems under varying information structures (static vs. dynamic). The core finding is that while projective quantum strategies yield optimal results in the static case, this optimality breaks down in the dynamic setting. This highlights the critical dependence of quantum advantages on the precise structure of information flow between agents.
main_problem: The research investigates how changing the information structure—from static to dynamic—affects the quantum advantage demonstrated by Bell inequalities in stochastic team games.
main_result: Projective quantum strategies, optimal for the standard (static) CHSH game, fail to maintain optimality when the information structure becomes dynamic, sometimes leading to a strict quantum disadvantage compared to classical strategies.
method: The authors model nonlocal games as team optimization problems, comparing minimum expected costs achievable by classical, projective, and general quantum strategies under both static and dynamic input conditions.
model_or_system: The primary model is the CHSH game analyzed through a team-theoretic lens. The analysis uses Bell states ($ho$) and characterizes strategies via Positive Operator-Valued Measures (POVMs) $\{E_i\}$ and $\{F_j\}$.
key_observables: Minimum expected cost ($E[B_1]$), quantum advantage (comparison of optimal costs across strategy classes), and the feasibility of best response properties.
important_parameters: Probability parameters defining the information structure, such as $(p_1, p_2)$ for static vs. $(p_s, p_c)$ for dynamic settings.
main_assumptions: The analysis often assumes that optimal quantum strategies can be characterized by their extreme points (e.g., rank-one projections) and compares the results derived from POVM optimization against action choices based on measurement outcomes ($u_i = m_i$).
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the comparison of fractional quantum advantage for both static and dynamic structures, showing how the region of quantum advantage shrinks or changes when moving to the dynamic setting.
paper_structure: The paper systematically compares team-optimal costs ($J^*$) across strategy classes (C, $\perp$, Q) for two distinct information structures (Static vs. Dynamic). It builds from general formulations to specific results regarding best response and person-by-person optimality failure in the dynamic case.
why_interesting: The findings demonstrate that quantum advantages derived under idealized static assumptions can be fragile and disappear when realistic, time-dependent correlations or noise channels are introduced into the information structure.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Fig. 1: A block diagram explaining the static and dynamic information structure. In the dynamic information structure the information and actions of the two players are interdepen- dent, unlike the static information structure.
Fig. 2: Fractional quantum advantage for static information structure:= (JS∗ C −JS∗ ⊥)/JS∗ C .
Fig. 3: Fractional quantum advantage for dynamic information structure.

Fig 3 shows the fractional quantum advantage for the dynamic information structure. Sim- ilar features like Fig. 2, such as maxima at the center and symmetry across quadrants, are observed in Fig. 3.
